chunk_summary: This section discusses medical supplementation for breastfeeding infants, including indications, recommended volumes, methods to protect breastfeeding skills, and strategies to return to exclusive breastfeeding.
learning_objectives: Understand medical indications for supplementation in breastfeeding infants.
Learn appropriate supplementation volumes based on newborn gastric capacity and clinical response.
Explore methods to deliver supplements while preserving breastfeeding skills.
Develop strategies to transition back to exclusive breastfeeding after supplementation.
key_facts: Supplementation is indicated for conditions like symptomatic hypoglycemia, excessive weight loss (>10%), dehydration, poor stool/urine output, and hyperbilirubinemia due to suboptimal intake.
Recommended supplementation volumes: Day 1: 5–10 mL/feed, Day 2: 10–15 mL/feed, Day 3: 15–30 mL/feed, Day 4 onward: 30–60 mL/feed, adjusted to clinical response.
Hierarchy of supplementation: mother’s expressed milk first, pasteurized donor human milk second, and formula only if human milk options are unavailable.
Supplement delivery methods include cup, spoon, syringe, paladai, or supplemental nursing system (SNS); avoid early bottle use to protect breastfeeding skills.
Aim for 8–12 milk removals per 24 hours during supplementation to protect and rebuild supply.
reference_quote: Supplementation should follow a hierarchy: mother’s own expressed milk first, pasteurized donor human milk second, and formula only when human milk options cannot meet needs. (ABM Clinical Protocol #3, Revised 2017)
document_key_topics: Medical indications for supplementation in breastfeeding infants
Strategies to protect breastfeeding skills during supplementation
